theme: The Invisible Layer — Sounds, Shortcuts, and Spatial Intelligence That Make the Office Feel Real Without Looking Different

ideas.title: Character-Specific SVG Avatars — Every Team Member Deserves a Face
ideas.description: The CharacterAvatar component in Office.jsx renders every non-Bloberto worker as the same generic gray blob with a colored body based on ROLE_COLORS. Carlos, Maya, Dave, Sofia, and Luna should each have unique SVG avatars that reflect their personality: Carlos gets dark hair, glasses, and a teal shirt (backend serious). Maya gets colorful streaks in her hair and a paint-stained top (creative energy). Dave gets headphones and a hoodie (DevOps vibes). Sofia gets a magnifying glass accessory and a sharp bun (QA precision). Luna gets silver-purple wavy hair and a crescent moon accessory (creative director). Each avatar is a 40x40 SVG, hand-drawn in the same art style as Bloberto — simple shapes, bold fills, 2-3 accent colors max. The CharacterAvatar function already switches on workerId for Bloberto — extend that switch to cover the 5 roster members. WHY: The office loses half its charm when 5 characters look identical. Unique faces make you care about who is working and who is idle. It transforms the Office tab from a status board into a cast of characters.

ideas.title: Keyboard Navigation — The Dashboard Should Be Drivable Without a Mouse
ideas.description: Add keyboard shortcuts for power users: Tab/Shift+Tab cycles focus through worker cards (each card gets tabIndex=0 and a visible :focus-visible outline matching the card accent color). 1 and 2 switch between Dashboard and Office tabs. Escape closes any open filter or tooltip. Ensure the tab toggle and theme button are keyboard-accessible with Enter/Space handlers. Add a tiny keyboard icon in the footer that on hover shows available shortcuts. WHY: Accessibility is not optional. A dashboard that can only be mouse-driven excludes keyboard users and power users.

ideas.title: Office Whiteboard — A Living Artifact That Shows the Team's Current Focus
ideas.description: Add a whiteboard element to the office floor — positioned at roughly left: 80%, top: 35%, sized 12% wide x 18% tall. The whiteboard is a rounded white rectangle with a slight shadow, containing dynamically rendered text: the count of active workers, the current vibe label, and the most recent activity log entry (truncated to 30 chars). Text is rendered as SVG text elements at small font sizes in a messy handwriting style. A dry-erase marker emoji sits at the bottom-right corner. WHY: Real offices have whiteboards covered in scribbles. This bridges the Office and Dashboard views — key stats appear visually in the office without needing to switch tabs.

ideas.title: Typing Indicator on Working Characters — Show That Work Is Happening
ideas.description: Working characters at their desks currently bob up and down via the bob animation. Enhance this by adding a tiny typing-dots indicator below each working character avatar — three 3px dots that animate in sequence with staggered opacity delays. Position absolutely below the avatar. The dots use the worker role color from ROLE_COLORS. WHY: The bob animation says movement. Typing dots say productivity. It is a universally recognized UI pattern from chat apps repurposed for a novel context.

ideas.title: Office Floor Plants — Decorative Life That Makes the Space Warmer
ideas.description: Add 2-3 small potted plant SVG elements scattered on the office floor at edges where no desks overlap. Each plant is a simple 20x28px SVG: a terracotta trapezoid pot with 3-4 green ellipse leaves. One could be a cactus variant. Add a subtle idle sway animation with a 6s duration. WHY: The office has furniture, people, and coffee but no life. Plants are the universal interior design shortcut for warmth.

ideas.title: Worker Card Expand/Collapse — More Detail On Demand Without Cluttering the Grid
ideas.description: Add a click handler that toggles expanded state on WorkerCard. When expanded, reveal full task description, hire date formatted relative, total session count from activity log, and last 3 activity entries inline. Only one card expanded at a time. Use a chevron icon to toggle. CSS max-height transition for smooth expand. WHY: The dashboard serves glancers and investigators. Expand/collapse serves both without compromise.

ideas.title: Easter Egg — Konami Code Triggers Bloberto Dance Party
ideas.description: Listen for the Konami code via a keydown sequence tracker in App.jsx. When triggered, add a dance-party class to office-floor for 5 seconds. All characters start dancing with a spin animation. Bloberto speech bubble shows a celebration message. A disco ball emoji appears at top center. After 5 seconds return to normal. WHY: Easter eggs are the secret handshake between a product and its most curious users. The Konami code is universally known in tech circles.

ideas.title: Ambient Particle Layer — Subtle Floating Motes That Give the Office Depth
ideas.description: Add a CSS-only particle layer to the office floor using a ::before pseudo-element. Generate 8-12 tiny circular box-shadows at random positions with a slow drift keyframe animation over 20s. Use white at 5-10% opacity in dark theme, darker motes in light theme. pointer-events none. WHY: The office floor grid is geometric and stark. Ambient particles add a subconscious sense of 3D space like dust motes in sunlight.

ideas.title: Responsive Office Layout — The Office Should Rearrange, Not Just Shrink
ideas.description: Instead of just scaling down on mobile, rearrange the desk layout on screens below 600px: switch from a 3x2 grid to a 2x3 grid, move the coffee corner to the bottom-right, reduce manager desk width. Compute a mobile DESKS array via useMediaQuery hook. WHY: Mobile visitors see a squished office where characters overlap. A responsive rearrangement preserves the spatial metaphor instead of destroying it.

ideas.title: Worker Thought Bubbles — Random Idle Musings That Make Characters Feel Human
ideas.description: Idle workers wandering the floor occasionally display thought bubbles (cloud-shaped, distinct from speech bubbles). Each roster member has 8-10 personality-appropriate thoughts: Carlos thinks about refactoring endpoints, Maya about parallax, Dave about automation, Sofia about bugs vs features, Luna about floating desks. Show randomly every 8-12s for 4 seconds. WHY: Idle workers are boring right now. Thought bubbles transform them from animated sprites into characters with inner lives.
